column1: In Java, what is the conceptual relationship between a class and an object?
column2: A class is a template or factory, while an object is a specific instance of that class.

column1: What is the primary purpose of a constructor in a Java class?
column2: To initialize the object by setting the initial values of its fields.

column1: What naming rule must all Java constructors follow?
column2: The constructor's name must be identical to the name of the class.

column1: What is the return type of a Java constructor?
column2: A constructor has no return type, not even void.

column1: What is the term for defining multiple constructors in a class with different parameter counts or types?
column2: Constructor overloading.

column1: What happens if a programmer does not define any constructor in a Java class?
column2: The compiler provides a default no-argument constructor that performs no additional operations.

column1: What does the keyword 'this' refer to within a class method or constructor?
column2: It refers to the current instance of the object.

column1: How can one constructor call another constructor in the same class to avoid code duplication?
column2: By using the syntax 'this(...)' as the first line of the constructor.

column1: In what specific position must a call to 'this()' or 'super()' be placed within a constructor?
column2: It must be the very first instruction in the constructor's body.

column1: When a constructor calls another constructor via 'this()', what is the first thing executed?
column2: The constructors for the parameters passed to the 'this()' call.

column1: What is the sequence of execution for a constructor that does NOT use 'this()'?
column2: 1. Parameter constructors, 2. Attribute constructors (initializers), 3. Constructor body.

column1: Concept: Encapsulation?
column2: Hiding the internal implementation details of an object and providing access through methods like getters and setters.

column1: Which visibility modifier is the most restrictive in Java?
column2: private

column1: What is the visibility of a field if no access modifier is specified?
column2: Default (also called package-private) visibility.

column1: Under package-private visibility, which classes can access the fields or methods?
column2: Only classes located within the same package.

column1: Which classes can access a member marked with the 'protected' modifier?
column2: Classes in the same package and all subclasses, even those in different packages.

column1: How does 'public' visibility affect access to a class member?
column2: The member is accessible to all classes regardless of their package.

column1: Are private attributes of an object accessible to other objects of the same class?
column2: Yes, private attributes are accessible from other instances of the same class.

column1: What is the correct hierarchical order of visibility from widest to narrowest?
column2: public $\succ$ protected $\succ$ default (package) $\succ$ private.

column1: In Java, what keyword is used to establish an inheritance relationship between classes?
column2: extends

column1: Does Java support multiple inheritance for classes?
column2: No, a class can only inherit from a single superclass.

column1: When a subclass object is created, which constructor is implicitly called first if not specified?
column2: The no-argument constructor of the superclass.

column1: What is the term for a method in a subclass that has the same signature as a method in the superclass?
column2: Method overriding.

column1: What annotation is used to indicate that a method is intended to override a superclass method?
column2: @Override

column1: What is a 'virtual method' in Java?
column2: A method that can be overridden by a subclass.

column1: Are private methods in Java considered virtual?
column2: No, private methods cannot be overridden and thus are not virtual.

column1: Concept: Polymorphism?
column2: The ability to treat an object of a subclass as an object of its base class.

column1: According to the Liskov substitution principle, what can always be substituted for a superclass object?
column2: An object of any of its subclasses.

column1: When overriding a method, how can the visibility range be modified?
column2: The visibility can be kept the same or made wider, but never narrower.

column1: If a superclass method is 'protected', what visibility can the overriding method in the subclass have?
column2: Either 'protected' or 'public'.

column1: What determines which version of an overridden method is called at runtime?
column2: The actual dynamic type of the object, not the static type of the reference.

column1: If a subclass defines an attribute with the same name as one in the superclass, which one is accessed via a superclass reference?
column2: The attribute in the superclass, as attribute access depends on the static type of the reference.

column1: How can a subclass access an overridden method or a shadowed field in the immediate superclass?
column2: By using the 'super' keyword.

column1: What are 'static' members in a Java class?
column2: Members that belong to the class itself rather than any specific instance
column3: they are shared among all objects of the class.

column1: Can static methods be overridden (i.e., are they virtual)?
column2: No, static methods are not virtual and cannot be overridden.

column1: What is the effect of the 'final' keyword when applied to a class?
column2: The class cannot be extended (it cannot have subclasses).

column1: What is the effect of the 'final' keyword when applied to a method?
column2: The method cannot be overridden by subclasses.

column1: What is the restriction on a variable marked as 'final'?
column2: Its value cannot be changed once it has been initialized.

column1: What is the difference between 'overloading' and 'overriding' regarding method return types?
column2: Overloading requires different parameters (return type alone isn't enough), while overriding requires a compatible return type (same or subclass).

column1: What keyword is used to declare a class that cannot be instantiated and may contain methods without bodies?
column2: abstract

column1: What must a non-abstract subclass do if it inherits an abstract method?
column2: It must provide an implementation for that abstract method.

column1: Is it permissible for a concrete method to call an abstract method within the same abstract class?
column2: Yes, this is a valid and common practice.

column1: What must happen to a class if it contains at least one abstract method?
column2: The class itself must be declared as 'abstract'.

column1: How do interfaces differ from classes in terms of their conceptual purpose?
column2: Interfaces define what a class can do (behavior), whereas classes define what a class is (identity/structure).

column1: Which keyword allows a class to adopt the behavior defined by an interface?
column2: implements

column1: Can a single Java class implement multiple interfaces?
column2: Yes, Java supports multiple inheritance only through interfaces.

column1: What are the implicit modifiers for all constants defined within an interface?
column2: public static final

column1: What is a 'default' method in a Java interface?
column2: A method that includes a default implementation, allowing classes to inherit it without mandatory overriding.

column1: What is the implicit visibility and nature of methods in an interface?
column2: They are implicitly 'public' and 'abstract' (unless static or default).

column1: Are 'protected' methods allowed in a Java interface?
column2: No, all methods in an interface must be public.

column1: What is the root class for all exceptions in Java?
column2: Throwable

column1: Which subclass of 'Throwable' is intended for serious system problems that should not be caught?
column2: Error

column1: What are 'checked exceptions' in Java?
column2: Exceptions (subclasses of Exception excluding RuntimeException) that must be either handled by a try-catch block or declared in the method signature.

column1: What defines an 'unchecked exception'?
column2: Any exception that is a subclass of 'RuntimeException'
column3: they do not require mandatory handling or declaration.

column1: Which keyword is used to explicitly throw an exception instance within a method?
column2: throw

column1: What is the purpose of the 'throws' keyword in a method signature?
column2: To declare a list of checked exceptions that the method might throw.

column1: When overriding a method, what is the rule for the 'throws' clause?
column2: The overriding method can declare the same exceptions, a subset (fewer), or subclasses of the original exceptions, but never more or broader checked exceptions.

column1: In a try-catch-finally block, which section is guaranteed to run regardless of whether an exception was thrown or caught?
column2: finally

column1: If multiple 'catch' blocks match a thrown exception, which one is executed?
column2: The first one that matches the exception type.

column1: How can a custom exception class be created in Java?
column2: By extending the 'Exception' or 'RuntimeException' class and providing a constructor.

column1: What is a Java 'collection'?
column2: An object used to store and manage a group of other objects with dynamic sizing.

column1: Which interface serves as the parent for List, Set, and Queue in the collection hierarchy?
column2: Collection<E>

column1: In the Java collection hierarchy, does the Map interface extend the Collection interface?
column2: No, Map is a separate hierarchy.

column1: What does the 'add(E e)' method in the Collection interface return?
column2: A boolean indicating whether the collection was changed as a result of the call.

column1: Which method is used to obtain an object for traversing the elements of a collection one by one?
column2: iterator()

column1: What method returns the number of elements currently stored in a collection?
column2: size()

column1: If a class 'B' extends 'A', and 'A' has a method 'm1()', can 'B' define a method 'm1()' with narrower visibility?
column2: No, this would cause a compilation error.

column1: Class A { void m() { print('A')
column3: Class B extends A { void m() { print('B')
column8: B (because methods are virtual and use dynamic dispatch).

column1: Class A { int i = 1
column3: Class B extends A { int i = 2
column8: 1 (because field access depends on the static type of the reference).

column1: When a constructor in class B calls 'super()', when are the attributes of class B initialized?
column2: After the superclass constructor finishes but before the rest of B's constructor body executes.

column1: Can an interface extend another interface?
column2: Yes, an interface can extend one or more other interfaces using the 'extends' keyword.

column1: What happens if a 'catch' block throws a new exception?
column2: The new exception will not be caught by the current try-catch block
column3: it must be handled by an outer scope.

column1: Can a 'try' block exist without a 'catch' block?
column2: Yes, provided it is followed by a 'finally' block.

column1: Is 'NullPointerException' a checked or unchecked exception?
column2: Unchecked (it is a subclass of RuntimeException).

column1: Is 'IOException' a checked or unchecked exception?
column2: Checked (it extends Exception but not RuntimeException).

column1: What is the purpose of 'super(s)' in a custom exception constructor?
column2: It passes the error message string to the parent 'Exception' class constructor for storage.

column1: Which standard collection interface forbids duplicate elements?
column2: Set

column1: Which standard collection interface maintains elements in a specific sequence and allows duplicates?
column2: List

column1: How can you check if a collection contains no elements without checking its size?
column2: By using the 'isEmpty()' method.

column1: What is 'shadowing' in the context of Java inheritance?
column2: When a subclass declares a field with the same name as one in its superclass, hiding the superclass field.
